1→---
     2→title: "Introduction - New Concepts"
     3→part: 27
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "3892-3899"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**NEW CONCEPTS**
     9→
    10→Innovation is the mother of invention. When Edison’s trial for lighting the bulb failed a hundred of times, people laughed. When Einstein discovered the gravitational theory, people laughed. People often laugh at new things thinking they are a joke. But eventually all such innovations become reality. 
    11→
    12→Therefore, try to innovate and find new ways to do things which are smarter, easier and shorter. Sometimes when you adopt new practices, people may not like to adopt or some may not like them because of sheer jealousy, but eventually people will adopt when they find them better in due course of time. You have to give time and explain about its benefits compared to earlier practices. There is a very good saying in English – “the only constant today is change”. So, change, change and change but change for the better so that employees would welcome and adopt such changes.
    13→
    14→I have picked up certain issues in the following chapters which can be subjected to introspection by you. You may like some, and not like some. But they give you some food for thought.
    15→

1→---
     2→title: "PROJECT HEALTH CARD"
     3→part: 27
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "3900-3935"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : PROJECT HEALTH CARD**
     9→
    10→Have you seen a job card maintained by the car maintenance engineer when you take a car to a workshop? Have you seen your own health card comprising results of your blood tests and other tests performed by the diagnostic laboratory?
    11→
    12→Similarly why not a Project Health card? Many would say what is new about this? MIS on Projects display various project data for managers to take necessary action. That’s 100% true. However, I would like to introduce some new parameters which I have not seen any MIS, yet I feel they are important indications to the health of a project. Let us discus one by one.
    13→
    14→**Time lapsed**   
    15→Suppose a high voltage transmission line project involves casting of 500 foundations, erecting of 500 tower structures and stringing (ie. Installing of overhead wires on top of the erected towers) of 499 spans between towers. Depending on physical time required, I put a weightage of 40% on foundations, 35% on tower erection and balance 25% on stringing.
    16→
    17→At any point of time, suppose 200 foundations are cast, 150 towers erected and 100 spans strung. Let us calculate the time lapsed.
    18→
    19→Time lapsed \= (0.4 \* 200/500) \+ (0.35 \* 150/500) \+ (0.25 \* 100/499)  
    20→		\= 0.16 \+ 0.105 \+ 0.126  
    21→		\= 0.391, ie. 39.10% of time taken 
    22→
    23→Suppose the completion period for the above project was 24 months.  
    24→Therefore time lapsed \= 0.391 \* 24 \=  9.384 months, say 9 months for simplicity
    25→
    26→Now, if the actual time spent from the date of award is 12 months to complete those 200 foundations, 150 towers. 100 spans of stringing, then your project is delayed by 3 months (12-9) because you have actually done a 9 month progress in a physical 12 month spent. But if the actual time spent from the date of award is 7 months, then your project is 2 months (9-2) ahead of schedule.
    27→
    28→The above example is a simple exercise with 3 activities only. You may list down more number of major activities (major meaning taking significant time) and do the exercise from time to time. This is a great way to determine the progress of a project broadly.
    29→
    30→**Money earned**  
    31→Every activity is associated with billing. Say foundations comprise of 25% of total price, tower erections 45% of total price and stringing balance 30% on total price.
    32→
    33→Taking the same example as above,  
    34→Money earned \= (0.25 \* 200/500) \+ (0.45 \* 150/500) \+ (0.30 \* 100/499)  
    35→     \= 0.10 \+ 0.135 \+ 0.06  
    36→                            \= 0.295, ie. 29.5% of total price 
    37→
    38→Therefore in 39.10% of time consumed, I have earned 29.50% of total contract price. In other words, I have spent more percentage of time than I have earned as a percentage of total contract price.
    39→
    40→It is obvious that the above two percentages cannot be same, however they should not differ much because the hardships and time consumed in executing an activity should be correlated to the price quoted for the activity. If the money earned % is far less than the time lapsed %, it means that the activities have not been quoted appropriately.
    41→
    42→Also intelligent bidders tend to front load the activities, which mean quoting more prices for activities to be done in the beginning and quoting lesser for activities to be done later. In this manner, you can better the money earned % with respect to time lapsed % meaning you earn more with respect to time. This leads the project to a better cash flow. However, there is a limit to which front loadings can be done otherwise the unit prices of the activities may seem to be unreasonable.
    43→

1→---
     2→title: "THE PRODUCTIVITY MEASUREMENT"
     3→part: 27
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "3936-3989"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE PRODUCTIVITY MEASUREMENT**
     9→
    10→We know Employee Turnover is the turnover, that is, revenues generated per employee. Here there is a catch. Should the number of employees include contractual employees. My answer is yes. If we understand the principle, contractual employees appointed in the name of the organisation, should be considered since they are wholly contributing to the turnover of the company. However, outsourced employees employed through third parties may not be considered but their CTC should be considered in calculating the employee overheads. Let me give you an example to illustrate interesting facts.
    11→
    12→Assume,  
    13→**SBU 1**  
    14→Turnover \= Rs 2400 Cr  
    15→Number of employees \= 1500  
    16→CTC of above employees \= Rs 141 Cr
    17→
    18→**SBU 2**  
    19→Turnover \= Rs 3400 Cr  
    20→Number of permanent employees \= 3000  
    21→CTC of above employees \= Rs 162 Cr
    22→
    23→**Calculation of companys’s employee turnover and overheads**  
    24→Total turnover \= 2400 \+ 3400 \= Rs 5800 Cr  
    25→Total employees \= 1500 \+ 3000 \= 4500  
    26→Employee turnover \= 5800/4500 \= Rs 1.29 Cr  
    27→Total CTC \= 141 \+ 162 \= Rs 303 Cr  
    28→Employee overheads \= 303/5800 \= 5.22%
    29→
    30→**Calculation of SBU wise employee turnover and overheads**  
    31→**SBU 1**  
    32→Employee turnover \= 2400/1500 \= Rs 1.60 Cr (better performed than SBU 2\)  
    33→Employee overheads \= Rs 141/2400 \= 5.875%  
    34→**SBU 2**  
    35→Employee turnover \= 3400/3000 \= Rs 1.13 Cr  
    36→Employee overheads \= Rs 162/3400 \= 4.76% (lower overheads than SBU 1\)
    37→
    38→**The concept of Employee Productivity Cost**  
    39→See the fallacy above. SBU 1 performed better in terms of productivity but had  
    40→higher overheads which is not desirable. Higher overheads may still lead to  
    41→better productivity, that is not the problem. The problem is confusion arises because of the number game, that is, the number of employees.
    42→
    43→Actually, the number should not matter. What matters is the total cost of employees and the turnover generated. A SBU may have a higher number of employees with lower salaries (which tends lower the Employee Turnover) as compared to a SBU having a lesser number of employees with higher salaries.  
    44→   
    45→So, let me introduce a different concept of Employee Productivity Cost, that is Turnover in lakhs generated per One lakh employee cost. This ignores the number of employees in the calculations.
    46→
    47→In the above example,  
    48→Employee Productivity Cost \= 5800 x 100/(303 x 100\) \= Rs 19.14 Lakhs  
    49→Employee Productivity Cost of SBU 1 \= 2400 x 100/(141 x 100\) \= Rs 17.02 Lakhs  
    50→Employee Productivity Cost of SBU 2 \= 3400 x 100/(162 x 100\) \= Rs 20.99 Lakhs
    51→
    52→What were the Employee Turnover calculated before?  
    53→Employee Turnover of SBU 1 was Rs 1.60 Cr and,  
    54→Employee Turnover of SBU 2 was Rs 1.13 Cr
    55→
    56→So Management would tend to believe that SBU 1 have done better than SBU 2 because their employee turnover is higher. But actually SBU 2 has done better because One lakh of employee cost of SBU 2 generated a turnover of Rs 20.99 Lakhs as compared to SBU 1 generating Rs 17.02 Lakhs. And since money is all that matters in a company so far the financials are concerned, the concept of Employee Turnover Cost is a better concept than the Employee Turnover.
    57→
    58→I am not writing off the concept of Employee Turnover. It does give a sense of the productivity in terms of turnover produced per employee but what I am trying to determine is that the Employee Productivity Cost is a better measure of employee productivity.
    59→
    60→To conclude, what figures of Employee Turnover, Employee Overheads and Employee Productivity Cost are good benchmarks? Well, that depends on the industry tou are in. You may calculate your figures vis-a-vis that of your competitors and arrive at benchmarks.
    61→

1→---
     2→title: "NON FINANCE FOR FINANCE"
     3→part: 27
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "3990-4001"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : NON FINANCE FOR FINANCE**
     9→
    10→Sounds absurd, no? We have all heard about Finance for Non Finance, that is providing Engineers (ie. Non Fianance) basic ideas about Finance. No denying the fact that Finance for Non Finance is very much required because engineers and all others not from the Finance fraternity needs to get it clear that as a part of organisation, that an organisations runs and flow with money, the business with which an organisation is engaged is all about money in terms of revenues, profits and cash flows. Therefore Non Finance individuals must be acquanted with the basics of Finance.
    11→
    12→But what about Non Finance (ie. concepts or basics of the engineering or the business that the organisation is engaged with) for Finance (ie. for Chartered/Cost Accountants, Finance MBAs, etc)
    13→
    14→Just like Engineers must have basics of Finance because organisation exists for the generation of money which engineers must understand, Similarly, Finance personnel must have the basics of the business they are in because organisation executes the business because of which the money is generated. So it is vice versa. With an understanding of the business, the Finance personnel gets a vivid idea about the profitability of the business, why advances and to what extent is required for such project business to run, requirement of bank guarantees and cah flow, etc. They would appreciate the requirements of all the aspects of Finance after knowing what the business is like and how it needs to be supported. Project execution is the “medium” to generate money. Project is the cause and Money is the effect. Until and unless Finance personnel appreciates the Cause-Effect mechanism, they will be operating in their own kingdom and in the process a project may be hampered due to certain Finance decision taken for the project.
    15→
    16→Therefore engineers and managers and Finance personnel must work in tandem with an underatnding of each other’s aspect and dynamics so that the organisation is successful.  
    17→
    18→Corporates must incorporate “Non Finance for Finance” training programs apart from the regular stereo typed “Finance for Non Finance” programs. I think understanding of business between the Finance and Non Finance people will increase substantially leading to coopertaion, unity and harmony during project execution.
    19→

1→---
     2→title: "GROUP ANTHEM AND GROUP DRESS"
     3→part: 27
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "4002-4009"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : GROUP ANTHEM AND GROUP DRESS**
     9→
    10→I feel proud when the Army or the Navy or the Air Force wears their designated dress. They surely feel proud to wear that dress and surely always feel proud to be a part of that force.
    11→
    12→Similarly, when we work in an organization, it is our duty to work for the organization and more importantly should feel proud to be a part of the organization. So why don’t we have a company dress in the organization. Is it a bizarre idea? I am not sure. But I have seen employees in factories wearing their company dresses. Then why not in offices?
    13→
    14→Similarly we surely feel patriotic when we sing the National Anthem. Then why not have a company anthem? Maybe it can boost the morale and unity amongst employees and foster harmony in the organization. The Anthem can be sung before every important occasion in the organization.
    15→

1→---
     2→title: "UNIFORM PAY STRUCTURE, POSITIONS AND RULES"
     3→part: 27
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "4010-4029"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : UNIFORM PAY STRUCTURE, POSITIONS AND RULES**
     9→
    10→HR frames rules and regulations for an organization. All employees in an organization need to follow these rules in letter and spirit. There are rules framed for Attendance, Dress code, Leaves, Benefits and facilities extended, Local, Domestic and Foreign tours, etc. for each Staff/Managerial position.
    11→
    12→Now, for example, the Tata’s, Birla’s, Goenka’s, Reliance’s, Adani’s, Mahindra’s, etc have lot of group companies. My point is why not the parent company of each group frames similar rules for its group companies. 
    13→
    14→Let me explain what I mean. The HR framework across all group companies can be the same. Its understandable that the strength and volumes of business of different companies of a parent company can differ widely. Therefore the framework can remain the same; however, the data can be tweaked to address any group company appropriately.
    15→
    16→Let me give examples. Suppose a parent company frames different grades of Managerial positions from M1 to M20; M1 having the least salary structure and M20 having the highest salary structure. Now the M1 grade salary may not be appropriate for a large group company whose turnover and profits are enormous. So such a company can incorporate managerial grades from M5 to M15, as you feel appropriate. Another small group company can adopt Managerial grades from M1 to M8, say as it suits them. And so on and so forth. In this system, the framework of the Managerial grades in different group companies can remain the same.
    17→
    18→Similarly, the concept can be extended to the positions associated with each managerial grade. However, framework for positions can be a challenge because a small sized group company will also be eager to keep a position like the Vice President (feel good factor) just as a large sized group company would also like to do so. But you can easily imagine that there is a lot of difference in the status and strength of a Vice President of a large sized company versus the Vice President of a small sized company. However, at least, the framework for the Managerial grades can be kept the same.
    19→
    20→Now, in the Managerial grades from M1 to M20, the rules pertaining to Leaves, Benefits and facilities extended, Local, Domestic and Foreign travels, etc can also be tagged to each Managerial grade with different applicable data. Some facility like Foreign Travel may not be applicable to a particular or range of managerial grades.
    21→
    22→The above concept can also be extended to the Staff grade.
    23→
    24→There may be challenges in adopting such a common framework of grades across group companies but the advantage of it is its uniformity. After all, every option has pros and cons; still we need to adopt a particular option in life if the positives of the option weigh more.  
    25→
    26→**ANNEXURES**
    27→

1→---
     2→title: "Introduction - Annexures"
     3→part: 28
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "4032-4033"
     6→---
     7→
     8→Annexures deals with subjects which are sometimes wrongly interpreted or less understood. Therefore I thought to discuss on some subjects in this Part to provide clarity to young professionals. However, you can delve on these subjects in various documents for a deeper understanding.
     9→

1→---
     2→title: "VISION & MISSION"
     3→part: 28
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "4034-4048"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**ANNEXURE: VISION & MISSION**
     9→
    10→Many employees are not clear what a Mission or a Vision of an organization is. Let us try to understand.
    11→
    12→Every organisation has a Mission and a Vision and they emphasize this to its employees and the outside world through a statement called the Vision and Mission statement.
    13→
    14→A mission statement defines the organization's business, its objectives, and how it will reach these objectives. A vision statement details where the organization aspires to go.
    15→
    16→Sometimes the terms “mission statement” and “vision statement” are used interchangeably or even combined into a single statement.  
    17→But they mean two very different things. Your mission statement is what your company is doing right now, while your vision statement is what you hope to achieve in the future – where you are in this moment versus where you’re going.
    18→
    19→Let me give a practical example. If someone asks you, “So, what do you do currently?” you might say, “I’m a Bidding Manager in a large-sized construction company”. But, what if they asked you, “What do you want to be in five or 10 years from now?” Your answer might be a bit different, right? Maybe you’d say, “My goal is to become the Strategic Business Head and ultimately would like to be the Managing Director of the organisation”
    20→
    21→In the above example, as a Bidding Manager, your Mission is to win many bids and have the largest market share but your Vision may be to head the business and eventually become the MD of the organisation.
    22→

1→---
     2→title: "DPR TO CONTRACTS CLOSURE"
     3→part: 28
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "4049-4092"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**ANNEXURE: DPR TO CONTRACTS CLOSURE**
     9→
    10→There are various stages of a project executed by a customer through its selected contractor. Let us understand the entire process step by step.
    11→
    12→1\. **DPR** \- Every customer studies in details the benefits of a project when implemented. It studies the requirements to build the project, ie. Land, cost of acquisition, permissions from different authorities, finance, manpower and equipment infrastructure and the timelines of construction. A Detail Project Report (DPR) is prepared comprising of above details which is subsequently approved by concerned authority.
    13→
    14→2\. **NIT** \- The customer publishes in brief, in newspapers, websites, etc., about the requirement of the project and invites prospective contractors to participate in the bidding for the project. This is called the Notice Inviting Tender (NIT) which also mentions the last date of procuring the bidding documents.
    15→
    16→3\. **Bid** \- Once the NIT is published, interested contractors purchase the bidding documents and prepare the bid. The last date of submission of the bid is also mentioned in the bidding documents. The bid generally has two parts, one is the Techno-commercial bid, which comprises of all technical and commercial documents required to be submitted in the bid, and the other is the Price or Financial bid which comprises of all Price Schedules detailing item wise and total prices with a Summary of Price Schedules. The compilation of all these documents is sealed in packets and submitted to the customer within the due date of submission is called the Bid. A bid needs to be valid for a period of time, could be three to six months, as prescribed. This means the bidder cannot change its prices or documents during this bid validity period.
    17→
    18→A bid has to be accompanied by a Bank Guarantee called the bid guarantee. This is a legal instrument for the customer which can be forfeited in the bank of the bidder in case (1) the bidder does not accept the bid corrections (as is discussed below) (2) the bidder withdraws its bid during the bid validity period. In such cases of withdrawal of bids, the bidder may be black listed for participating in future bids of the customer.
    19→
    20→4\. **Bid opening** – Once bids of various bidders are submitted, the opening of the bids can be on a single part or on a two part basis. In a single part bid, both the Prequalification bid and Price bid of all bidders are opened in front of representatives of all bidders and brief particulars submitted in the bids are announced including the Total Bid Price quoted. In a two part bid, only the Techno-commercial bid of all bidders are opened and brief particulars submitted in the bids are announced.
    21→
    22→5\. **Bid evaluation** – Evaluation of the bids is undertaken. Firstly, evaluation of Techno-Commercial Bids is undertaken to scrutiny if the submissions are in line with the requirements of the bidding documents. Bids which meet these requirements are called responsive Techno-commercial bids. Bidders submitting non responsive Techno-commercial bids are rejected and their Price bids are not evaluated though being announced on the bid opening date.
    23→
    24→Thereafter, in a single part bid, the Price bids are scrutinised for responsiveness for responsive Techno-commercial bidders only. Bidders submitting non responsive Price bids are rejected. Amongst the responsive Price bids of responsive Techno-commercial bidders, the least priced Bidder is declared as L1, the next L2 and so on. In a two part bid, the bidders who submitted responsive Techno-commercial bids are notified for the opening of their Price bids on a particular date and thereafter the process of Price bid evaluation and finalisation of L1, L2, etc. is undertaken in a similar manner as described above.
    25→
    26→A important note on Bid evaluation criteria. Most bids in India are based on least priced bids as described above. For a bid to be techno-commercial responsive, you just need to meet the minimum requirements stipulated in the specifications. All bidders who are responsive techno-commercially are treated equally. And then their Price bids are evaluated.
    27→
    28→However, in more stringent bids, many such in the international domain, the evaluation stipulates weightage of each bid. For example, a Techno-commercial bid may have a 60% weightage and the Price bid may have 40% weightage. In such a case, apart from meeting minimum requirements, the bidder whose technical and commercial particulars submitted are much of higher standards than others are rewarded by getting more points due to the high weightage. Even if the bid is not the least priced tender, the bidder may get the award if it scores high in the techno-commercial bid. **Such awards are not based on least priced bids.**
    29→
    30→6\. **Bid clarification** \- Small errors in the Techno-commercial bid and the Price bid can be corrected by the customer and notify the bidder for their acceptance. The customer can also seek for missing documents from the bidder. If a bidder cannot provide missing documents or do not accept to the logical corrections in the Price bid, the customer has the right to reject such bidders.
    31→
    32→6\. **Pre-award session** – The L1 bidder is invited for a pre-award session where the L1 bidder is asked to re-assure fulfilling the important conditions of the bidding documents. The contractor also confirms to provide required bank guarantees upon award and jointly with the customer draw up a item wise time lines of project execution. This broad timeline drawn in line with completion period of the project is called the L1 network. All these discussions are recorded to produce a Minutes of Meeting (MoU) signed off by both the customer and the L1 bidder.
    33→
    34→7\. **Letter of Intent (LoI**) – This step is not mandatory. Sometimes the customer proceeds directly to the next step. However, in some cases, the customer informs the L1 bidder of its intent to award the project. This is called the LoI which generally mentions the total contract price, if awarded.
    35→
    36→8\. **Notification of Award (NOA)** – The L1 bidder is invited and the customer places the award through a letter called the Notification of Award (NOA). NOA may also be called a Award, Order or a Contract. It is at this stage that a bid is called a Contract and the L1 bidder is now known as the Contractor. The NOA also comprises of the bidding documents, the Techno-commercial bid and the Price Bid, and the MoU of pre-award session.
    37→
    38→9\. **Project Execution** – Based on the terms and conditions laid out in the Contract, the project is executed by the contractor. On the basis of the L1 network, the contractor draws up a slightly detailed item wise timelines called the L2 network. The L2 network is discussed and approved by the customer. In some cases, further minutely detailed networks such as the L3, L4 and L5 networks are also drawn up.
    39→
    40→10\. **Project Completion** – Based on the detailed networks, the project is completed physically and handed over to the customer for pre-commissioning tests.
    41→
    42→11\. **Pre-commissioning** – Pre-commissioning means operational tests which are conducted to ensure successful operation of the project.
    43→
    44→12\. **Commissioning** – The successful pre-commissioned project now commences operations.
    45→
    46→13\. **Contracts closure** – It includes reconciliation of materials to be supplied to the customer including spare parts as per Price Schedules and contract vis-a-vis actually supplied. Similarly, it also includes reconciliation of finances, payment to receive from customer as per Price Schedules and actual payments received. The differences are noted and accordingly transactions between the customer and the contractor are undertaken to nullify the differences.
    47→
    48→13\. **DLP** – Defect Liability Period (DLP) is typically a period of 12 months from the date of physical completion of the project or 18 months from the last supplied made to the project, whichever is later, during which the contractor is liable to rectify all defects pertaining to supply and services rendered during the execution of the project. Such rectifications have to be undertaken by the contractor at it’s own cost. Even if the supplies or services were accepted by the customer during project execution, the contractors must rectify the same during DLP if similar or other defects arise in the same supplies or services.
    49→
    50→A short note on Latent Defects Period (LDP) versus DLP. Sometimes, an inherent defect does not show up within a DLP of one year due to technical reasons. For example, a roof of a building may have been cast with inferior materials or services and the leakage during monsoon shows up after a period of three years. Such inherent defects, though not initially visible or show up in a DLP of one year, are called Latent Defects. Some clients also put a clause of LDP of 10 years besides stipulating DLP of one year. In a LDP too, the contractor must rectify these latent defects even if similar or other defects did not arise during DLP. Such latent defects must be reactivated by the contractor at it’s own cost. Due to such LDP clause, contractors have to ensure very good quality of construction; otherwise the contractor may have to incur losses in the long run.
    51→

1→---
     2→title: "JOINT VENTURE PARTNERSHIPS AND PRE-BID TIE UPS"
     3→part: 28
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "4093-4115"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**ANNEXURE: JOINT VENTURE PARTNERSHIPS AND PRE-BID TIE UPS**
     9→
    10→If you have read the Annexure on “DPR to Contracts Closure”, you know how a customer evaluates the Techno-commercial bid of bidders. You need to meet the minimum techno-commercial requirements specified in the bidding documents. **This is called the Pre-Qualification criteria, PQ criteria i**n short.
    11→
    12→PQ criteria stipulate requirements in different areas. The bidder may be required to have completed a minimum number similar projects in the past (Experience based PQ), to have manufactured a minimum quantity of a material or product (Manufacturing based PQ), to possess a minimum number of a particular construction equipment (Equipment based PQ), to have a Minimum Average Annual Turnover (MATT) and a minimum amount of Liquid Assets certified by a Chartered Accountant (Finance based PQ), to have a minimum number of qualified engineers in different disciplines to be posted in the project in case of an award (HR based PQ)
    13→
    14→Now the issue is a bidder may satisfy one PQ but not the other or some parts of different PQ. Therefore he plans to rope in a partner where the partner can fulfill those PQ or parts of PQ which the bidder fails to fulfill. Such an arrangement is called a Joint Venture (JV). The bidders, two or more in number, are called the JV partners. The JV is committed through a common understanding which forms the basis of an agreement called the JV Agreement. In a JV agreement, the details include the different scope of work of the JV partners and other terms and conditions by which all JV partners shall abide. The bid submitted by the JV is called a JV bid which must also include the JV Agreement to be scrutinised by the customer.
    15→
    16→JV bids are usually allowed by customers. However please note that the customer can put riders on different PQ such as, each JV partner must fulfill at least 25% of Experience based PQ, 50% of Finance based PQ and so on. In some other kinds of PQ like HR based PQ, there may be no riders.
    17→
    18→Searching for the JV partner is a tough exercise of the Marketing Department. It not only has to ensure that the partner fulfills the required PQ which it cannot fulfill on its own as well as that the partner so selected is competitive. Fulfilling both the objectives which are mandatory to win a contract is really a difficult job since such partners may not be found in plenty in the market. Also partners who joined hands with other bidders in the past sometimes form a “permanent” kind of association and therefore such partners are unlikely to join hands with a new bidder until that partner wishes to opt out from its earlier arrangement.
    19→
    20→Many JV partnerships fail across the globe. Mistrust and misunderstanding crop up which are the reasons of partnerships being broken. Trusting and creating a win-win situation while drafting JV Agreements satisfying all partners is a necessity for a JV to sustain. A successful JV stands a long way for the success of organisations in the long run since the same partners can hold hand in hand and win many future contracts.
    21→
    22→**Exclusive Pre-bid tie ups**  
    23→Exclusive partnerships with a particular vendor for a selected time period or for a particular bid are called a pre-bid tie up. In an exclusive pre-bid tie up, the vendor is not allowed to give any kind of offers to other competitors for the stated time period or the bid and the contractor too, on award, cannot place an order to any other similar vendor other than the pre-bid tie up vendor. 
    24→
    25→Therefore a Pre-bid tie up works for a vendor if it thinks that the contractor has a high chance of winning and therefore the vendor offers it’s best possible prices to the contractor. 
    26→
    27→Such a pre-bid tie up also works well for a contractor if it thinks that it has high chance of winning a bid by receiving the best possible prices from the vendor.
    28→
    29→Without a pre-bid tie up, a vendor does not generally offer best prices because it keeps its price escalated to keep a cushion for future negotiations. The contractor knows this and therefore in a pre-bid tie up it knows that the vendor have already released all its negotiation margins and have offered the best possible prices. Thus on award, generally, the contractor has no room to negotiate with the vendor further. Such a clause may already be stipulated in the pre-bid agreement between the contractor and the vendor.
    30→

1→---
     2→title: "LIQUIDATED DAMAGES & CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES"
     3→part: 28
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "4116-4129"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**ANNEXURE: LIQUIDATED DAMAGES & CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES**
     9→
    10→Liquidated damages are defined as a specified amount of money to be compensated for a breach of contract. The clause could be as follows:
    11→
    12→Liquidated damages (LD) shall be 0.1% of contract value per week of delay or PART THEREOF subject to a maximum of 5% of contract value. In other words, if a contractor delays a project by some period of time due to no fault of the client, then 0.1% of contract value shall be deducted per week of delay from the contractor. 
    13→
    14→Two important points are to be noted; 
    15→
    16→1) Note the phrase PART THERFOF, which means even a part of the week. Most employees get this wrong. If I asked what will be the damages for 10 days of delay, many answers 0.1% x 10/7 \= 0.14%, ie. They follow the unitary method. But it is not so because 10 days of delay will be considered as 2 weeks of delay, hence the liquidated damages shall be 2 x 0.1% \= 0.2%  
    17→2) The deduction shall be on contract price, which may be a huge sum, until some contract mentions damages to be considered on balance unexecuted portion of the contract value. Therefore this is an important point of concern. 
    18→
    19→Consequential damages are a very dangerous clause. That is, if there is a delay of the contractor, then the client shall default in its service to its customer for which the customer can levy a huge penalty on the client. This penalty will be passed on by the client to the contractor which is called the consequential damages. Such damages may wipe off your entire profits of the project and that is why this is a dangerous clause to accept.
    20→

1→---
     2→title: "PART OF BID SUBMISSIONS"
     3→part: 28
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "4130-4148"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**ANNEXURE: PART OF BID SUBMISSIONS**
     9→
    10→Some employees get confused on part bid submissions. Bids comprises of two parts; the Techno-Commercial bid containing documents of all technical and commercial requirements and Price bid containing the Price Schedules and the Summary Price.
    11→
    12→Two part single opening may follow two methods; (1) both the parts will be opened in one go, thereafter client will take time to evaluate responsiveness of techno-commercial bid and thereafter prices will be evaluated for only the responsive techno-commercial bids and eventually declare the position of the bids, L1, L2 and so on based on price bid responsiveness (2)  both the parts will be opened in one go, the prices will be announced; thereafter client will take time to evaluate responsiveness of techno-commercial bid and subsequently prices will be evaluated for only the responsive techno-commercial bids and eventually declare the position of the bids, L1, L2 and so on based on price bid responsiveness. So you may have been declared L1 but eventually you may not be the evaluated L1.
    13→
    14→Two part two opening means only the techno-commercial bid will be opened first and then the client will take time to evaluate responsiveness of techno-commercial bid, thereafter the Price bids shall be opened on another date for only responsive Techno-commercial bids and these price bids will be evaluated eventually declare the position of the bids, L1, L2 and so on based on price bid responsiveness. In this case, the bidder cannot modify their price bid since it remains submitted to the client right at the beginning.
    15→
    16→In both cases, non-responsive techno-commercial bids and non-responsive price bids are rejected and are not considered for award. In such cases the bid guarantees submitted by the bidder are returned to the customer in due course of time. 
    17→
    18→Another important point to be noted on the forfeiture of bid security. Bid security can be forfeited under the following circumstances;
    19→
    20→1) If the bidder withdraws its bid during the bid validity period  
    21→2) If the bidder does not accept the corrections made by the client on its techno-commercial bid and price bid where mistakes have crept in  
    22→3) If the bidder does not accept award from the client based on its submitted techno-commercial and price bid.  
    23→4) If the bidder does not accept client’s request to withdraw the deviations provided by the bidder
    24→
    25→Also please note on an important cross breach clause. Sometimes a client splits an award to a contractor in two parts; the Techno-commercial contract and the Price contract. In such cases, breach of any contract will be automatically deemed to be a breach on the other contract. This is known as the cross breach clause.
    26→

     2→title: "DEFECTS LIABILITY PERIOD AND LATENT DEFECTS PERIOD"
     3→part: 28
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "4149-4154"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**ANNEXURE: DEFECTS LIABILITY PERIOD AND LATENT DEFECTS PERIOD**
     9→
    10→Many employees are not conversant with the concept of Latent Defect. Defects Liability Period (DLP) is the period after a contract is commissioned during which the contractor has to provide warranty to remedy defects or replace the defective goods or services. Typically DLP is 12 months from the commissioning of the contract or 18 months from the last supplies made in the contract, whichever is later.
    11→
    12→Latent Defects Period (LDP) is the period after a contract is commissioned during which the contractor has to provide warranty to remedy or replace defects which show up after considerable period of time. An example of this is poor quality casting of roof of a building in which case seepage may show up after several years of withering monsoons. Therefore these defects are those which are inherently present but do not show up during the DLP. Typically LDP may be 10 years from the commissioning of the contract.
    13→

1→---
     2→title: "FRONT LOADING IN PRICE BIDS"
     3→part: 28
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "4236-4264"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**ANNEXURE: FRONT LOADING IN PRICE BIDS**
     9→
    10→This is a bidding trick adopted by many bidders while finalising Price Bids. Let me explain what it is by considering a simple Price Schedule of 3 items only.
    11→
    12→**Item description		Qty	Unit	Unit Price	 	Total Price**
    13→
    14→1\. Survey of land		1	Lot	Rs 40,000		Rs 40,000  
    15→2\. Supply of Product A	10	Nos	Rs  25,000		Rs 250,000  
    16→3\. Installation of A		10	Nos	Rs 5,000		Rs 50,000
    17→
    18→**TOTAL								Rs 340,000**
    19→
    20→Assume, as per specifications, Item 1 will be executed first 3 months, item 2 from 4th to 10th month, and item 3 in parallel from 6th to 12 month. You are supposed to receive payments as per these timelines in line with above Price Schedule. Also assume that these were your best prices for submission. Now the bidder front loads the Price Bid as follows:
    21→
    22→**Item description		Qty	Unit	Unit Price	 	Total Price**
    23→
    24→1\. Survey of land		1	Lot	Rs 140,000		Rs 140,000  
    25→2\. Supply of Product A	10	Nos	Rs  18,000		Rs 180,000  
    26→3\. Installation of A		10	Nos	Rs 2,000		Rs 20,000
    27→
    28→**TOTAL								Rs 340,000**
    29→
    30→Interestingly, please note that the total price, which was your best price to be submitted remains the same. However, you have “front” loaded price in item 1 for which will be executed in the first three months itself and payments received subsequently. So you get lot of cash much before than actually you would have if you had not front loaded.
    31→
    32→There are certain issues of such front loading. Let us discuss.  
    33→1\. If you front load to much which looks totally unreasonable, the customer may object or reject your bid as non-responsive.
    34→
    35→2\. There will be disparity between cost and price of different items. Like when you get paid for item 1, the profit margin is exceedingly high. When you get paid for item 2, the profit margin may be Zero or slightly negative. When you get paid for item 3, the margins are highly negative. However, the total intended margins remain the same. Sometimes Finance Department does not try to understand the same and makes a hue and cry when they find losses while executing item 3\. But they don’t take a note of the fact that item 1 was executed with exceedingly high margins and that the total intended margins remains the same, and most importantly, they should be glad of a advance Cash Flow situation. This is why situations like these can be covered in “Non Finance for Finance” courses which I have suggested for.
    36→

1→---
     2→title: "PAYMENT TERMS AND BANK GUARANTEES"
     3→part: 28
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "4265-4313"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**ANNEXURE: PAYMENT TERMS AND BANK GUARANTEES**
     9→
    10→A part of a Contract deals with Payment Terms. A typical Payment terms and the associated Bank Guarantees is discussed below:
    11→
    12→**Advance Payment**  
    13→A contractor needs Advance Payment for mobilising his Designs activities and preliminary works in a project site. So a typical Advance Payment could be 10% of contract value to disburse by the customer against (1) furnishing the Invoice for the same (2) furnishing an Advance Bank Guarantee of like amount and (3) furnishing a Performance Bank Guarantee of 10% of Contract value.
    14→
    15→Advance Bank Guarantee is required by the customer to secure itself from cases of contractor running away with the Advance money so disbursed. This Guarantee remains valid till the completion of project. However, the value of this Bank Guarantee may decrease by 25% when project is executed and the balance work to execute is 75% of Contract value. It can further reduce by another 25% when the project is further executed and the balance work to execute is at 50% of Contract value. In this manner, the value of this Bank Guarantee can be reduced up to 25% when balance of Contract value to be executed is 25% or lesser. Value of this Bank Guarantee cannot be reduced further.
    16→
    17→Performance Bank Guarantee is required by the customer to secure itself from the non-performance of the contractor. In case the contractor does not perform as per timelines after repeated warnings, then such a Bank Guarantee can be forfeited and the Bank, without requiring any explanations of the contractor, can forfeit the money and hand it over to the customer. This Guarantee remains valid till the completion of DLP.
    18→
    19→**Progressive Payment**  
    20→Progressive payments, usually monthly, are paid by the customer against submission of bills by the contractor. Important point to note is that while the bill amount of each item is for the full value of the item as quoted in the Price Schedules, the contractor will be paid for 80% of the value only because 10% is the advance already paid earlier and 10% is the retention (discussed hereafter).
    21→
    22→**Retention Payment**  
    23→The retention money of 10% could be payable on completion of the project by furnishing Retention Bank Guarantee for like amount. This Bank Guarantee shall be valid till the DLP.  In case the contractor agrees to receive the retention money at the end of DLP, then no such Bank Guarantee will be required.
    24→
    25→Bank Guarantees are legal instruments for the customer for compensation against the default of contractor for which the Bank Guarantee is being taken. In case of default of contractor even after several warnings for rectification, the customer can present the Bank Guarantee established by the contractor to the bank of the contractor asking for forfeiture of the guarantee, in other words, the amount of the Bank Guarantee is paid to the customer from the bank of the contractor. While disputes may arise in case of forfeiture, banks are obliged to make such payments to the customer without any permission of the contractor though it is the money of the contractor. The bank can, however, notify the customer of such forfeiture. Such is the power of Bank Guarantees.
    26→
    27→Another Bank Guarantee called the bid guarantee, submitted during bid submission, has already been discussed in another Annexure.
    28→
    29→**ANNERXURE : MAX CAP**
    30→
    31→This is a interesting concept which can be introduced in an organisation. We are all human beings and have finite capabilities. It depends on what limits of capability the Almighty has given us and also depends on the hard and determination of a individual. If someone tries as much as he can and is determined to do well and has all the facilities, he still may not be able to become another Einstein or a Sachin Tendulkar. It is said such geniuses are God gift.
    32→
    33→Of course the example I gave is an extreme. However, in a similar say, you can predict that a Manager can at most perform in the levels of a VP depending on the qualification, experience, talent, potential, current designation, determination, hard work, etc. This could be his maximum level of performance called the “Max Cap”.
    34→
    35→Max Caps are predictions. They can go wrong sometimes, but yes, they give you an idea as to how much you can look for growth.
    36→
    37→Now let me raise the main issue with max cap. Suppose a engineer at 50 is a General Manager and have reached his max cap. He has another 10 long years to go before retirement. So as he works for some more years, he expects to be promoted to Vice President. But actually say, his limited capability does not allow him to perform as desired from a VP level.
    38→
    39→These cases are to dealt by HR in a sensitive manner. Such max caps need to be discussed at the beginning itself. Of course the HR can give a rider while discussing someone’s max cap that this is judgment at this point of time. In case the candidate shows much better potential and performance later on, his max cap can be changed.
    40→
    41→So, if executives are aware of their max cap, they will not have a grudge once they reach the maximum capability level. In case he feels his max cap can be raised higher, he can always discuss with HR later on.
    42→
    43→**ANNEXURE : MEANING OF INDUSTRY**
    44→
    45→This is simple to understand. All organisations together catering to the same product or service is called an industry. For example, Samsung, LG, OnePlus, Vivo, etc is the mobile manufacturing industry. Airtel, BSNL, Vodaphone, etc belongs to the mobile services industry. Bata, Khadim, Sree Leather, etc belongs to the shoe manufacturing industry. Nike, Adidas, Puma, etc caters to the sports shoe industry. Hero, Atlas, Kross, etc belongs to the bicycle manufacturing industry. Jio, Airtel, Hoichoi, Zee05, etc caters to the OTT service industry
    46→
    47→Each industry may have several manufacturers or service providers. They are basically competitors of that industry. Each has a market share in the total industry size.
    48→
    49→**ANNEXURE : MARKET ENTRY COSTS**
    50→
    51→Existing players in a industry face competition amongst each other. No existing player would like the entry of a new player because it will increase the competition further. However, a new player sincerely wants to do business in a particular sector. The new player also needs to gain practical experience through actual implementation of projects/ contracts. However, it also knows that the established players have lot of experience and cost optimisation techniques to submit competitive offers and bids.
    52→
    53→Therefore, a bidder, in sheer determination to surely win a contract, may consider Nil as profits or even negative sometimes (which means in case of an award the company would incur loss) or consider lesser overheads of the company than it actually is, to lower the cost of bid as much as possible.
    54→
    55→Such lowering of costs are called Market Entry costs. In other words you need to pay a price to enter a market. After all, nothing comes free on this earth\! Even the “Buy 2 get one free” strategy of marketers either gains little profit or no profit by giving one item free, but that is the price it pays to increase its market share.
    56→

1→---
     2→title: "LAND COMPENSATION & ROW"
     3→part: 28
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "4314-4331"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**ANNEXURE: LAND COMPENSATION & ROW**
     9→
    10→In India or some other countries, acquiring land, which is required execute a project, is a very big challenge. If the land belongs to a farmer or a private entity and the government needs the land for building a project, then several scenarios posing challenge to the government emerge. If the land required is linear in nature and therefore not required to be purchased, then the construction agency needs the rights to access the path for constructing the project. This right to access is called the Right of Way (ROW).
    11→
    12→1\. The owner might not be at all ready to give away the land at any cost. In some case, He even says that the land is like his own child and refuses to budge away even against lucrative offers.
    13→
    14→2\. The owner might not agree to the amount of compensation and the government is bound by certain laws for compensation. Hence the situations remain stalemate for years.
    15→
    16→3\. The owner might even say that there are 500 mango trees in my land and each tree yields 200 mangoes in one year. So in the next 50 years, he will lose a yield of 500 x 200 x 50 \= 50 Lakh mangoes for which the government should also compensate apart from the cost of land. Such reasons lead to stalemate again.
    17→
    18→4\. The land owner may also ask for alternate land in lieu of the land he sacrifices. This alternate land may be mush costlier than the land owner sacrifices. Such claims may also lead to a stalemate.
    19→
    20→5\. Sometimes, the government needs to look for alternate lands or routes which may increase the cost of the project.
    21→
    22→With lot of persuasion, alternate arrangements or increase compensation, a government entity finally may manage to get the land required for the project. But the agency loose time and money to build the project and the facility that the public would have enjoyed gets delayed.
    23→
    24→In view of above challenges, sometimes the government announces tenders where the land compensation and ROW is in the scope of the contractor. The contractors are not bound by any laws as to limits of compensation, so they are free to negotiate and compensate required amounts. However, the bidder has to take care that if it assumes high costs of compensation unnecessarily, then its bid cost will be high is unlikely to win. The catch is the compensations must be judged to certain accuracy so that the cost is realistic, neither high nor low. However, the land owners, at the time of bid, know that the project is under consideration and is not being built currently. So they may indicate reasonable costs. But afterwards when they find that a contractor has been awarded and now has to acquire the land for construction, they ask for hefty compensation which was not envisaged during pre-bid survey. This often poses a big challenge for the contractors to contain costs within the bid costs considered at the time of bid submission.
    25→

1→---
     2→title: "LEVEL OF SCHEDULES"
     3→part: 28
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "4332-4359"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**ANNEXURE: LEVEL OF SCHEDULES**
     9→
    10→**Schedule** is a time management tool that identifies tasks, events, or actions for an entire time frame with a scale and sequences of activities. The Schedule is a list of planned activities to be done showing the times or dates that is estimated by other information included resources, budgets, activities duration, and linkages of dependencies, etc.
    11→
    12→**Project Schedule** is a time, cost and resource control and management tool that identifies activities for entire project with time scales, required budget and resources, and relations with dependencies. The Project Schedule is a plan for completion of a project, list of planned activities to be done within allowed time frame, usually with intended start and finish dates that serves as a tool to assist project management in achieving project goals through efficient use of available resources.
    13→
    14→**Level of Schedules** is a Project Master Schedule (Level 1), Project Summary Schedule (Level 2), Control Level Schedule (Level 3), Detailed Network Schedule (Level 4), and Reports Schedule (Level 5). The Master or Project Summary Schedule is one of the contract documents, and the Control Level Schedule is a project baseline document.
    15→
    16→**Level 1 Schedule**: L1 Schedule is the Executive Summary, also called a Project Master Schedule (PMS). This is a major milestone type of schedule; usually consists of only one page, and it highlights major project activities, milestones, and key deliverables for the whole project. It is used to summarize the project schedule in reports and other documents when a more detailed schedule is not required. Frequently developed by the ‘Client’ as part of its initial feasibility studies for the project then maintained by the contractor; may be used to assist in the decision-making process (go/no-go, prioritization, and determining the criticality of projects). It can be used to integrate multiple contractors / multiple project schedules into an overall program schedule. Audiences for this schedule Level include (but are not limited to) Clients, Senior Executives, and General Managers. If included with a bid and/or the Contract, it demonstrates conformance to contractual and other milestones. When it comes to Oracle Primavera P6 Level 1 schedule, it will be shown as one bar only. This bar summarizes the project data as per the below photo.
    17→
    18→**Level 2 Schedule**: L2 Schedule is the Management Summary, also called a Summary Master Schedule (SMS). It is maintained as a summarization of the Level 3 Project Coordination Schedule(s). It represents the overall project as the project is broken down into its major components, and is used for higher-level management reporting. Frequently developed by the ‘Client’ as part of its commitment plan for the project and then maintained by the contractor. It can be used to integrate multiple contractors / multiple project schedules for the overall control of a program. It will include the Level 1 information expanded to show activities and major items of capital equipment. It should demonstrate the driving path for structures and major process systems at the CSI Division Level (e.g., Earthwork, Foundations, Framing, Enclosure, MEP Services, and Process Equipment), based on the project method statement. Audiences for this type of schedule include (but are not limited to) General Managers, Sponsors, and Project Managers. Oracle Primavera P6 Level 2 schedule as shown in the below figure will open the level 1 and level 2 of your schedule.
    19→
    20→**Level 3 Schedule**: L3 Schedule is the Project Coordination Schedule (PCS) and also called Publication Schedule. Initially developed as an integrated CPM (Critical Path Method) overview of the project, the Level 3 schedule is then maintained as an integrated roll-up or summary of the Level 4 schedule activities for reporting status to Senior Management and to report monthly status to major project parties, etc. The schedule consists of a set of integrated Level 4 schedules; these schedules are based on Critical Path Methodology (CPM) and developed with detailed input from the project management team. It is usually developed by the ‘Main Contractor’ as a part of its tendering process for the project or by the project team during the initial phases of planning. The Level 3 schedule spans the whole of the project and is used to support the monthly report. It includes all major milestones, major elements of design, engineering, procurement, construction, testing, commissioning, and/or Operation. If the Level 4 schedules are primarily developed by trade/sub-contractors, during the initial phases of the project, the Level 3 schedule provides the schedule framework and constraints used by the subcontractors to develop their tenders. During the execution phase of the project, this schedule defines the overall critical path and it is the primary coordination tool for the overall project. Audiences for this type of schedule include (but are not limited to) Project Managers, CMs or Owner’s Representatives, Superintendents, and General Foremen. The below figure shows the level 3 schedule on Oracle Primavera P6
    21→
    22→**Level 4 Schedule**: L4 Schedule is the Execution Schedule, also called Project Working Level Schedule. Level 4 is the detailed working level schedule, where each schedule is an expanded part of a Level 3 schedule and is established within the integrated project schedule. This is the key working-level CPM schedule displaying the activities to be accomplished by the project workforce and is required for every project. The dates generated by the scheduled activities represent the anticipated start and completion of work required to complete the project. If there is no Level 3 schedule, activities in future months/years may be summarized but still provide approximate start and completion dates for major pieces of work (this is called ‘rolling wave scheduling’). Developed by the ‘Contractor’, ‘Subcontractor’ (trade contractor), or the project team before starting work on the project execution, or work in a phase or area of the project. The Level 4 schedule may be for the whole project or a part of the project depending on the size of the project and complexity of the work. A critical factor is keeping each Level 4 schedule to a sensible size that can be easily managed, updated, validated, etc. Level 4 schedules may be used for major sections of the work or discrete processes such as a ‘Design Schedule’, ‘Procurement Schedule’, and/or a ‘Commissioning Schedule’. Generally, the Level 4 schedule represents the area of authority of a section manager or engineer, so one manager is responsible for all the work in the schedule. Activities are generally over a week in duration (depending on the nature of the project) and should be resource loaded at least in the near term and include detailed crew movements and other means and methods to ensure viability. When needed, short-term ‘look ahead’ are produced from this level; typically, Three Week Lookahead schedules are updated every two weeks. Audiences for this type of schedule include (but are not limited to) Project Managers, Superintendents, and General Foremen.
    23→
    24→**Level 5 Schedule**: L5 Schedule is the Detailed Schedule. It is a further breakdown of the activities of a Level 4 Schedule. The schedule is used to map out the detailed tasks needed to coordinate day-to-day work in a specific area. Level 5 schedules are developed by workforce supervisors to plan and coordinate their work at the detail level; Workarounds and critical areas can be explored here. Typically, in Bar Charts (Gantt Charts) The schedules are replaced every 1, 2, or 4 weeks depending on the complexity of the work, typically schedules extend for 1 or 2 weeks beyond the date when the replacement is due (ex: a weekly schedule will extend for 2 weeks and be updated/replaced each week), this encourages continuity of working. Audiences for this type of schedule include (but are not limited to Superintendents, Team Leaders, Crew Leaders, General Foremen, Foremen, and Sub-foremen.  
    25→**General Notes:**  
    26→\- When available, the WBS is used as a basis for the development of the schedule structure.  
    27→\- Level 1 and 2 schedules are normally developed as part of the pre-feasibility studies to determine the viability of the project.  
    28→\- Only Mega projects will have fully maintained Level 1 and Level 2 schedules. A smaller project typically only has a Level 2 schedule.
    29→
    30→\- The Level 3 schedule is initially developed as a high-level CPM overview of the project. If the project is relatively small, the Level 3 schedule is expanded into a Level 4 schedule for coordinating the execution of the works. On larger projects with multiple Level 4 schedules, the Level 3 schedule is maintained as the project’s overall, integrated CPM schedule.
    31→
    32→\- Every project requires a Level 4 schedule for day-to-day coordination of the project’s work. However, the overall size of this schedule needs to be ‘manageable’ and focused on the work in one management area. The Level 3 schedule becomes critical for the overall coordination of the project as soon as more than one Level 4 schedule is in use. 
    33→

     2→title: "BID COMMITTEE & LEVELS OF APPROVAL"
     3→part: 28
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "4360-4376"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**ANNEXURE: BID COMMITTEE & LEVELS OF APPROVAL**
     9→
    10→A bid submission is of immense importance to an organization. The future of many organizations depends on the bid. Winning bids relates to expected turnover and profits of the company, the type of prequalification criteria it adds to its repertoire, the experience that the employees will have during execution of the projects. The entire welfare and future of the organization heavily depends on the bids.
    11→
    12→On the contrary, a grave mistake in a bid which has been won spells out disaster to the company. It previously accumulated profits may be washed out, it may damage the reputation and financial strength of the company which may lead to curtailment in employee benefits and so on and so forth.
    13→
    14→Also a bid has multi-dimensional aspects. There are engineering, costing, contracts and financial aspects to a bid. Therefore it is a good idea to form a Bid Committee of internal experts from the domain of engineering, costing and finance. All bids intended to be submitted must go through a multi-level approval process of this Bid Committee.
    15→
    16→Let me explain the levels of approval.   
    17→**Level 1**: It is an in-principle Bid-No bid decision process. In this process, the significant terms and conditions of the bid including estimated ball park price has to be illustrated by the Bid Manager with the support of Engineering, Costing, Contracts and Finance Managers. If approved by the Bid Committee, then the Bid moves to Level 2 approval process. Otherwise, the bid is rejected for submission
    18→
    19→**Level 2**: It is a techno-commercial approval process. The terms and conditions are detailed further. The risks are noted with necessary mitigation process including incorporation of appropriate costs against the risks enumerated. The Bid Committee may reject the bid if it finds that the risks being taken are dangerous and too difficult to take at that point of time. If approved, the bid moves to Level 3 approval process.
    20→
    21→**Level 3**: It is a Price bid approval process. The Bid Manager is expected that the costs evaluated for all supplies, services, project management, financial and overheads are correct and checked. The Bid Committee will generally have no time to check thousands of costs considered during the preparation of a bid. A major decision to be taken at this level is the profit margins to be loaded based on anticipated competition and the philosophy of the organization itself.
    22→
    23→At this stage itself, the Bid Manager may take approval for a further reduction of the profit levels to be passed on as discount to the client during reverse auction process, if called for by the client after the price bid opening. In a reverse auction process, you can try to be the least priced bidder and win a project even if you are not L1 in the price bid opening.
    24→

1→---
     2→title: "GOOD WINS and BAD WINS"
     3→part: 28
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "4377-4396"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**ANNEXURE: GOOD WINS and BAD WINS**
     9→
    10→When I first assumed the position of Head of Business Development, I was very happy on my first win in a bid where there were a dozen of competitors. However, I found that my Managing Director was not happy. I could not understand why. After some introspection and feedback I understood the issues. And that is when I first learnt about good wind and bad wins.
    11→
    12→My company in the bid was L1 amongst 12 bidders but the difference in price bid between us (L1 bid) and next L2 bid was 7%. In other words, I could have increased our bid price for another 6.9% and yet become L1. However, such statements are easily said than done. 
    13→
    14→In a competitive bid, you don’t know at all what other competitors are likely to quote. It is like traversing in a dark space with no navigation tools. And, today, unlike the yesteryears, price bids are not finalized entirely by the estimated costs. Today, a new bidder may quote a low price, maybe with loss, to enter the market and experience execution of a project, a bidder may quote higher than usual anticipating that other bidders are also likely to quote high given the difficult terrain to execute the project, a bidder can quote low if it has received a very low exclusive price from a vendor for a major item, so on and so forth. So, that is why it is said bid submission is like traversing in the dark without tools. In spite of all odds, you still need to anticipate the competitors intention, his likely quote, etc and finalise your price bid.
    15→
    16→A good win is if you are L1 but with only up to 3% lower than L2. If the difference is around 5%, you can claim it as a moderate win. And if it is more than 5%, no doubt that you win but it can be termed as a bad win.
    17→
    18→These percentages are not sacrosanct, you can define these percentages appropriately on your own, however, they are just for your reference to understand what a good and a bad win is. 
    19→
    20→There is a joke amongst many bidders, but please don’t follow them though sometimes it is proved to be true. That is, some bidders say, “if you make inadvertent mistakes in a bid, then only you can win”. In other words, if you price your bid carefully and correctly, you will lose. Sounds funny, yet sometimes proven right.
    21→
    22→There is also another debate about your performance in winning bids. How many bids you win to be labeled as a good performer? After all, your competitors are no fools; some may be more intelligent and capable than you.
    23→
    24→There are many parameters to this answer. If you are a very big and a strong organization, the Management will expect more wins so that it commensurate with the size of business. It also depends as to how many bidders are generally in the race in the bids. 
    25→
    26→As a thumb rule, if you are a moderate sized organization and there are more than dozen bidders in the fray, a 10% market share (ie. One out of every 10 bids) is considered as a good performance. Again, you can put your own figure as it suits your organization, but this 10% is only for a general reference. If the number of bidders is much less, then obviously this percentage would be more.
    27→

1→---
     2→title: "THE HITLERIAN AND GANDHIAN STYLE OF LEADERSHIP"
     3→part: 28
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "4397-4414"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**ANNEXURE: THE HITLERIAN AND GANDHIAN STYLE OF LEADERSHIP**
     9→
    10→This is an interesting article I read in a newspaper long time back and I found it so very appropriate in our corporate and personal life.
    11→
    12→Like in our democratic country, you will also find democracy in corporates. In other words, you will find many committees formed in a corporate to take various important decisions. This resembles democracy. 
    13→
    14→So in critical situations of decision making where you have time, you can approach the Gandhian style which basically means to take views of important employees who have adequate knowledge in the subject. In this style, you don’t resort to conflicts and peacefully hear out everyone to take a decision as a leader. In any particular decision, you shall find many pros and cons, however if in a option the pros weighs much more than the cons, then you need to take that decision. So in this style of leadership, you take a democratic view of the few who have expertise in the subject.
    15→
    16→What about the Hitlerian style? I will give you an example. A bid was approved by a tender committee with 6% profit margin and the bid was submitted accordingly. On evaluation, the bid turned out a L1 bid. All employees were overjoyed which was cut short by the client when it announced that all the bids will have to undergo a reverse auction process to determine the least priced bid. The reason for this was that the client’s estimate was still lower than the L1 bid and therefore the L1 bid could not be accepted as it is. Before the reverse auction date, the bid was again put up to the Bid committee to allow for a 1% reduction in the profit margin so that the 1% of total price could be passed on as discount during the reverse auction.
    17→
    18→When the reverse auction started, bidders (without knowing the identities, as is the case in reverse auction), started giving discounts and counter discounts and our discount of 1% of bid price was exhausted. We were in a fix on what to do next. Just sitting on it will render our bid as lost. We tried to connect with the MD and other Bid committee members, but they were all extremely busy in very important meetings.
    19→
    20→As it happens, few tells me to withdraw because there is no management approval of further discount but few tells me that winning the project is very important for the future of the company in terms of acquiring Pre-Qualification criteria. In such a mental turmoil, you as the Head need to keep your mind cools, understand the consequences of withdrawing or providing more discounts against the last management approval for the sake of the company. You have to take your decision right away without further hearing anyone. I went for another 1.5% discount and won the reverse auction process. This was a Hitlerian (dictator) style of leadership.
    21→
    22→The entire matter was reported to the Bid committee post facto with a presentation on the benefits of winning the project even at the cost of 1.5% lesser profit margins. The benefits far outnumbered the lesser margins and the bid submission after the reverse auction was approved post facto.
    23→
    24→Sometimes in your corporate life or even in your personal life, you have to take decisions in a dictatorial style. You may later on see that the decision was wrong, but in most case I found them to be right. When you can weigh the advantages of a decision quickly and time is short, take decisions dictatorially with your instincts and do not follow a democratic process which requires time.
    25→

1→---
     2→title: "MISCONCEPTS OF PERMANENT POSITIONS"
     3→part: 28
     4→section: 5
     5→original_lines: "4415-4424"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**ANNEXURE: MISCONCEPTS OF PERMANENT POSITIONS**
     9→
    10→In the yesteryears, I remember many trying for a job in the government because it was “permanent”. They knew once they are employed in a permanent position, it is very unlikely for them to get removed by the organisation in future till retirement. In other words, their job and financials were secured.
    11→
    12→That concept of being permanent still holds good today also though it is basically meaningless. I will explain you why.
    13→
    14→Many organisations recruit employees on a Contractual basis, say for a particular project just awarded to the company. He feels uncomfortable that since the position is not permanent he may be removed from the company at any point of time or when the project is completed if there are no more projects to serve.
    15→
    16→However, such a thought has a flaw. Today, every permanent employee have in its employment terms and conditions that either the company can remove you with one months’ notice or you may resign from the company by providing one months’ notice without citing any reasons. Therefore, for whatever reasons, if the company wants to remove you at any point of time or after the completion of a project, the company can do so without any obligations or providing reasons. So, what was the use of being permanent? Whether permanent or contractual, company can remove any employee in line with the employment terms.
    17→
